title: Polymer clay sculpted hedgehog
subtitle: A small realistic hedgehog figurine baked in a domestic oven
excerpt: A small hedgehog figurine in polymer clay, built over an aluminium foil armature to reduce weight and oven-curing time. The body is textured with a needle tool to suggest spines; the face and paws are sculpted separately. Hardens at 130°C for 25 minutes.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay figurine techniques are widely practised in the craft community. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A PVC-based modelling material that sets permanently in a domestic oven at 110-130°C. Not a true ceramic; the cured piece is a hard, durable plastic.
The oven-baking process that hardens polymer clay. The temperature and duration are set by the clay manufacturer.

content.text: Polymer clay sculpts smoothly and holds fine detail well, making it well suited to small animal figurines. The
polymer clay

hedgehog in this tutorial is built on a foil core that reduces the clay volume, which shortens the curing time and avoids uncured centres in thick sections. No wheel, no kiln: the piece hardens in a standard domestic oven. Do not use the same oven tray or tin for food after curing polymer clay.

attrs.content: Use a dedicated oven liner or tray for polymer clay. Do not share with food-prep cookware. Cure at the manufacturer's stated temperature; burning polymer clay releases fumes.

items.name: Polymer clay, brown
items.substitution: Beige or grey for a lighter colouring

content.text: Making the foil core

Crumple a piece of aluminium foil into an oval body shape about 50 mm long and 30 mm tall. This is the armature. Pack it firmly so it does not collapse when clay is pressed over it. The hedgehog body will sit slightly tilted, with the nose end lower than the back.
Covering with clay
1. The back spines area

Condition 40 g of brown polymer clay by kneading it until it is soft and pliable. Press it over the top two-thirds of the foil core (the back and sides) in an even layer about 5 mm thick. Smooth the surface with the rib.
2. The belly
Press 10 g of cream polymer clay over the underside and lower flanks of the form. Blend the edge where it meets the brown clay by pressing and smoothing along the join line so there is no hard seam.
Sculpting the face

Roll a small ball of cream clay and press it onto the front of the body for the snout, blending the edges. Push a small pointed nose of brown clay onto the tip of the snout. Add two tiny balls of black clay for the eyes, pressing them gently into the head on either side of the snout. The eyes should sit about 15 mm apart and 8 mm above the snout tip.
Texturing the spines

Work across the brown back area with the needle tool, pressing short lines into the surface in rows from the front of the spine area toward the rear. Each press should be about 3 mm long and 1 mm deep, angled from front to back, simulating a spine. Work in rows from side to side. On a real hedgehog, spines point backward and slightly outward from the centre; follow that pattern. This takes about five minutes and is the most satisfying part of the build.
Adding the paws

Roll four small sausages of cream clay about 10 mm long and 5 mm wide. Press two under the front of the body as front paws and two under the back for hind feet. Mark two or three short toes on each paw with the needle tool.
Curing
Preheat the oven to 130°C. Place the hedgehog on a dedicated lined tray.
Cure

for 25 minutes. The foil core means the internal temperature reaches the cure point faster than a solid clay piece would. Remove from the oven and allow to cool on the tray; do not handle until cool to the touch.
Finishing

The cured piece is strong and the clay colour will not fade. Apply a thin coat of matt varnish or acrylic sealer over the back to enhance the spine texture. The belly can stay as the natural cream clay colour. A dot of black acrylic paint on the eyes adds a realistic shine.
Variations
Make a sleeping hedgehog by curling the form into a tight ball with the face barely visible. No paws needed; texture the whole surface with spines.
Add a tiny mushroom or leaf under one paw to create a woodland scene. Mushrooms are a simple pinch-pot shape in orange and brown clay.
Scale up to 80 mm using a larger foil core and more clay per layer. Increase cure time to 35 minutes for the larger mass.

items.cause: Clay was not fully conditioned before building, leaving dry spots that split under heat
items.fix: Knead the polymer clay until it feels soft and even throughout before sculpting

items.symptom: Eyes sink or fall off after curing
items.cause: Eye balls were too small and not pressed firmly enough into the head
items.fix: Use slightly larger eye balls pressed firmly. Flatten them very slightly so they sit flush rather than sitting proud on the surface

items.symptom: Spine texture looks too flat after curing
items.cause: Lines were not pressed deep enough
items.fix: Each spine mark should be at least 1 mm deep. Go over the back with the needle tool again after initial texturing to deepen
